channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Russian Vape Compromise Faces First Hurdles
item.description: Russia’s proposed compromise on vaping regulation is facing early challenges as policymakers continue to weigh regional restrictions against broader market controls. Business groups and harm reduction advocates warn that fragmented bans could weaken legal trade, encourage illicit supply and reduce access to lower-risk alternatives for adult smokers. The debate highlights ongoing tensions between youth prevention, enforcement capacity, taxation interests and evidence-based approaches to nicotine regulation. Questions remain over whether a regional pilot model can produce meaningful public health benefits without repeating the unintended consequences seen in more prohibition-focused markets. (Published on May 28, 2026by2firsts.com)

item.title: A ridiculous but revealing conference declaration
item.description: The European Conference on Tobacco or Health 2026 in Milan aimed at advocating for strict regulations and measures to control tobacco and nicotine products. The declaration suggested making cities nicotine-free, enforcing uniform regulations on all products, and enhancing price policies. The text also emphasized protection of non-smokers, integrating control measures into urban policies, and investing in cessation services. However, critics argue that the declaration overlooks harm reduction strategies, obstructs access to safer products for smokers, and lacks consideration for individual choices. The conference's focus on enforcement, industry interference, and disregarding harm reduction leads to skepticism about its real impact on public health. (Published on May 28, 2026byclivebates.com)

item.title: It’s time for a new approach to quit smoking, once and for all
item.description: Australia’s smoking cessation strategy is facing renewed scrutiny as experts call for a more pragmatic approach to helping smokers quit. Critics argue that current policy has become too restrictive, particularly around vaping, while combustible cigarettes and illicit tobacco remain widely available. The debate highlights growing concern that limiting access to lower-risk nicotine alternatives may undermine cessation efforts for people who have not succeeded with conventional methods. Questions remain over whether Australia’s public health framework can better balance prevention, medical support, harm reduction and real-world smoker behaviour. (Published on May 28, 2026bymedicalrepublic.com.au)

item.title: Illinois’ Latest Flavor Ban Proposal Ignores Falling Youth Vaping and Smoking Rates
item.description: Illinois’ latest flavour ban proposal is adding to debate over whether nicotine policy is keeping pace with current youth use trends. Critics argue that falling rates of both youth vaping and smoking should prompt more careful assessment of whether broad flavour restrictions are proportionate or evidence-based. Harm reduction advocates warn that limiting flavoured vaping products may reduce the appeal of lower-risk alternatives for adult smokers while doing little to address illicit supply. The debate reflects wider tensions between youth prevention, adult smoking cessation, consumer behaviour and the need for regulation grounded in reliable population-level data. (Published on May 29, 2026bythr101.org)

item.title: Cancer and non-cancer risk analysis of vaping. Part I
item.description: Risk analysis of nicotine and tobacco products requires a clear distinction between combustible smoke exposure and non-combustible forms of nicotine delivery. Evidence on cancer and non-cancer risks continues to show that the greatest health burden is linked to toxicants produced through combustion, rather than nicotine itself. Researchers argue that comparing products through relative risk, exposure levels and disease pathways is essential for avoiding misleading conclusions in public health communication. The analysis reinforces the importance of evidence-based regulation that recognises meaningful differences between cigarettes, vaping products and other smoke-free nicotine alternatives. (Published on May 29, 2026bysubstack.com)
